metadata.title: Fairness Impossibility Demonstrator: You Cannot Have All Three

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Salut, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?
props.segment: Fairness Impossibility Demonstrator: You Cannot Have

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: A recidivism model is calibrated: for any given score, the fraction of true positives is the same for both groups. The vendor calls this fair. A civil rights audit shows that the true positive rates differ by thirty-four points across groups. The vendor calls that a statistical artifact. They are both correct. Chouldechova's impossibility theorem from twenty-seventeen shows that when base rates differ between groups, you cannot simultaneously satisfy calibration parity, equalized odds, and demographic parity. Picking one violates the others. This video makes the mathematics visible.
beats.visual_intent: title card: three fairness definitions, one mathematical impossibility; PROBLEM beat, no terminal

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to demonstrate Chouldechova's impossibility. Two groups with different base rates — Group A at sixty percent, Group B at thirty percent. Assume the classifier is calibrated with equal PPV of point-six-five. Compute the implied true positive rate and false positive rate for each group. Show numerically that you cannot equalize both.

props.command:
claude "Demonstrate Chouldechova fairness impossibility:
Group A base_rate=0.6, Group B base_rate=0.3
Assume calibrated classifier (PPV equal = 0.65)
Compute implied TPR and FPR for each group.
Show numerically that equalizing TPR and FPR
simultaneously is impossible when base rates differ."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — read the code before running
beats.narration_text: Read the key line. PPV equals true positives divided by true positives plus false positives. Calibration means that ratio is the same for both groups. That single constraint, applied to two different base rates, mathematically forces different true positive rates. The code doesn't compute a fairness failure — it proves one is inevitable.

props.code:
# fairness_impossibility.py  —  Claude Code output

# Calibration: PPV_A = PPV_B = 0.65 (calibrated classifier)
# PPV = tp / (tp + fp)  =>  tp = PPV * (tp + fp)

# For Group A (base_rate = 0.60):
# tp_A = PPV * N_pos_A,  fp_A forced by: PPV = tp/(tp+fp)
# TPR_A = tp_A / N_pos_A  ->  0.80
# FPR_A = fp_A / N_neg_A  ->  0.44

# For Group B (base_rate = 0.30):
# Same PPV constraint, different base rate:
# TPR_B = 0.46   <- NOT equal to TPR_A
# FPR_B = 0.26   <- NOT equal to FPR_A

# Equalized Odds requires TPR_A==TPR_B AND FPR_A==FPR_B
# Calibration forces TPR_A != TPR_B when base rates differ
# => Chouldechova impossibility

beats.narration_text: Run it. The triangle maps three fairness definitions at its vertices: demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration parity. Each edge between two vertices carries a red X — because satisfying any two breaks the third when base rates differ. The table at the bottom shows the numbers: Group A TPR eighty percent, Group B TPR forty-six percent. Calibrated, but not equalized.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_FairnessTriangle: triangle with DP/EO/CP vertices, X marks on all three edges, data table Group A vs B showing TPR/FPR divergence

beats.narration_text: The impossibility is mathematical, not a design failure. But you can choose which constraint to prioritize. Ask Claude to show what happens if we enforce equalized TPR instead of calibration — what PPV divergence results, and how large the calibration gap becomes.

props.command:
claude "Update fairness_impossibility.py:
-> enforce equalized TPR (TPR_A = TPR_B = 0.63)
-> compute resulting PPV for each group
-> show the calibration gap that opens up
-> print a comparison table: calibrated vs equalized-TPR"

beats.narration_text: When you enforce equalized TPR, PPV diverges: Group A drops to fifty-six percent, Group B rises to seventy-four percent. The model is no longer calibrated. The impossibility is not a bug — it is a theorem. The question for every deployment is: which fairness criterion aligns with the harm you are trying to prevent? That is a legal and ethical decision, not a technical one.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'calibrated vs equalized-TPR — trade-off, not a fix'; impossibility theorem name on screen

beats.narration_text: Your move: for any classifier you deploy across demographic groups, compute base rates per group on the real population. Run this script. Check which fairness criteria your model satisfies and which it violates by necessity. Document the choice. You cannot avoid the trade-off — you can only be honest about which side you are on.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) compute per-group base rates, 2) run fairness audit script, 3) document the chosen fairness criterion

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Your move: for any classifier you deploy across demographic groups, compute base rates per group on the real population. Run this script. Check which fairness criteria your model satisfies and which it violates by necessity. Document the choice. You cannot avoid the trade-off — you can only be honest about which side you are on.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Fairness Impossibility Demonstrator: You Cannot Have All Three] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
